macro_segments.title: Introduction and Context: Placement of the Mishnah on Yevamot 24b within the Broader Discussion

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and review of previous Mishnah about two brothers and two women
Explanation of why the discussion on primary obligation of yibum was inserted and interrupted the flow
Transition to current Mishnah on marriages that should not happen but if they do, are permitted to remain

macro_segments.title: Mishnah 24b: Cases of Suspected Liaison with Forbidden Women—Non-Jewish Woman or Slave Who Converted or Was Freed

micro_segments.title: Case 1: Man suspected of affair with slave who is freed; she is forbidden to marry him lechatilla but marriage stands b'dieavad
Case 2: Man suspected of affair with non-Jewish woman who converts; she is forbidden to marry him lechatilla but marriage stands b'dieavad
Distinction from married woman case: rationale for permitting these marriages b'dieavad—no inherent issur if the rumor were true

macro_segments.title: Mishnah 24b: Case of Suspected Liaison with a Married Woman—Requirement for Divorce Even B'dieavad

micro_segments.title: Case 3: Man suspected of affair with married woman who is divorced; marriage to him requires divorce even b'dieavad
Explanation of why married woman case is more stringent: if affair were true, she would be forbidden to him biblically

macro_segments.title: Gemara Discussion on Conversion with Ulterior Motives: Rav Ashi's Principle and R. Nehemiah's Position

micro_segments.title: Gemara's question: if conversion is valid, why forbid the marriage even lechatilla?
Baraita on conversions with ulterior motives: Gerey Arayot, Gerey Chalomot, Gerey Mordechai v'Esther—all invalid until converted in times without incentive
R. Nehemiah's position: conversions motivated by fear, economic benefit, or external pressure are not valid gerim
Halakha: we do not rule like R. Nehemiah; conversions with ulterior motives are valid b'dieavad
Introduction of Rav Ashi's principle: remove from yourself the perversity of the mouth—avoid reinforcing rumors

macro_segments.title: Extended Discussion on Geirus: Motivation, Acceptance of Mitzvot, and Contemporary Application

micro_segments.title: Distinction between lechatilla and b'dieavad regarding conversion for marriage; contemporary practice and teshuvot
Reference to teshuvot by contemporary poskim (e.g., R. Shlomo Kluger) addressing conversion motivation in modern times
Discussion of whether refusal to convert forces intermarriage and the lesser-of-evils calculus in b'dieavad reality
Concern that converting for marriage while planning to violate halakha (not getting married) undermines kabbalat mitzvot

macro_segments.title: Gemara on Acceptance of Converts in Various Historical Periods and Conditions

micro_segments.title: We do not accept gerim in the time of Moshiach, or in times of David or Solomon when conversion was motivated by material benefit
R. Elazar's proof from pasuk: one who converts must do so without me, without the obvious reward
Tosafot's resolution: Gitti and Bat Paroh converted in Solomon's time, but they were already wealthy so no ulterior motive
Discussion of Gibeonites in David's time and other cases of gerim grurim (converts dragged along) accepted b'dieavad despite questionable motivation

macro_segments.title: Gemara on Evidence for Adultery and Forced Divorce: The Dispute Between Rav and Rav Sheshet

micro_segments.title: Rav's position: forced divorce from the accused man requires eidim (direct witnesses) to the affair
Rav Sheshet's objection: Rav must have been asleep when he said that; baraita shows forced divorce without witnesses in certain conditions
Baraita scenario: woman suspected of affair with Reuven, marries Shimon, divorces Shimon; Reuven may then marry her without forced divorce
Logic: if the rumor is interrupted by her marrying another man, Reuven can marry her; this proves no eidim required, only rumors matter

macro_segments.title: Rav's Response and Clarification: No Eidim Required for Divorce Only in Absence of Children

micro_segments.title: Rav maintains position: only eidim require forced divorce; but acknowledges baraita may assume case with children present

macro_segments.title: Baraita on Conditions for Forced Divorce Without Eidim: Cases with and without Children

micro_segments.title: When is forced divorce enforced without witnesses? Only when she has no children from first marriage
If she has children from first marriage, do not force divorce to avoid reinforcing rumor and questioning children's paternity
With eidim of tumah (witnesses to adultery), force divorce even if she has children; paternity concern is overridden

macro_segments.title: Rav's Response and Clarification: No Eidim Required for Divorce Only in Absence of Children (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rav's explanation: the baraita's language of hotzi'uha (they removed her) suggests court force, implying eidim must be present

macro_segments.title: Rava's Reinterpretation: The Baraita Speaks of Forced Divorce from First Husband, Not Forced Divorce from Second Husband

micro_segments.title: Rava questions why baraita uses language of forced removal from first husband; this implies court action which requires eidim

macro_segments.title: Rav's Response and Clarification: No Eidim Required for Divorce Only in Absence of Children (continued) (continued)

micro_segments.title: Resolution: Rav is not debating halakha, only interpreting the mishnah's language; no real disagreement with baraita

macro_segments.title: Rava's Reinterpretation: The Baraita Speaks of Forced Divorce from First Husband, Not Forced Divorce from Second Husband (continued)

micro_segments.title: Conclusion: baraita assumes case with eidim and children; Rav agrees the halakha but interprets baraita differently

macro_segments.title: Gemara Resolution: Reconciling Rav and Rebbe—Rumor That Ceases vs. Rumor That Does Not Cease

micro_segments.title: Both positions are correct in different scenarios: kala d'lo pasik (rumor that doesn't cease) follows Rebbe; kala d'pasik (rumor that ceases) follows Rav
Definition of kala d'lo pasik: rumor must persist for a day and a half continuously; Abbaye's teaching from his nursemaid
Interruption in rumor only counts as real cessation if not motivated by fear; if people stop due to fear, it's not a real break
Rumor only creates basis for divorce if the parties have no enemies; enemies may fabricate rumors for ulterior motives

macro_segments.title: Tosafot's Crucial Distinction: Rumor Standard Applies Only to Second Husband, Not to Forced Divorce from Original Husband

micro_segments.title: Tosafot (Rabbeinu Tam): the entire sugya discusses whether to force divorce from the suspected man if she marries him, not from her original husband
A woman is only forbidden to her original husband by kinui and setirah (sotah process) or by direct witness to intercourse, not by rumor
The baraita's cases of suspicious evidence (milkman leaving, spit on canopy, overturned shoes) only apply to second husband, not forced divorce from first

macro_segments.title: Mishnah on Remarriage After Divorce: Cases Where Man May Not Remarry His Ex-Wife

micro_segments.title: Man who divorces wife because of shem ra (rumor of affair) may not remarry her
Man who divorces wife because of neder (her vow he could not tolerate) may not remarry her
Rationale: preventing remarriage ensures husband cannot later claim the divorce was conditional on the rumor/neder being true
If he knows there is no remarriage possibility, he cannot claim he only divorced due to the rumor; his intent must be deeper

macro_segments.title: Gemara Debate: Whether B'dieavad Remarriage Is Permitted After Forbidden Divorce

micro_segments.title: Question posed: if remarriage is forbidden, and it happens b'dieavad, must they divorce?
Attempted proof from adultery case: if unmarried to adulterer requires forced divorce b'dieavad, so too remarriage after forbidden divorce
Counter-distinction: in adultery case, marriage itself reinforces the rumor; in remarriage case, there is no similar negative consequence

macro_segments.title: Gemara Conclusion: B'dieavad Remarriage Is Permitted Despite Rabbinic Prohibition

micro_segments.title: Final ruling: remarriage after forbidden divorce is permitted b'dieavad, unlike the adultery case
Reasoning: remarriage shows the original rumor/neder was false, countering sociological harm; no deeply problematic union like adultery
Broader observation: most cases in the mishnayot are permissible b'dieavad; only the adultery case requires divorce b'dieavad

topical_tags.term: Rav Ashi's principle: Haser mimecha ikshus peh
michol b'shfoferet (applicator in eyelash tube—graphic description of intercourse)
Gerey Mordechai v'Esther (converts during Purim events)
Gerim Grurim (converts dragged along against will)
Rabbeinu Gershom (Medieval authority on monogamy and divorce)
hotziuha (court forced removal from first husband)
kala d'lo pasik (rumor that does not cease)
